course_description:
The course introduces a systems approach to develop chemical engineering processes through process analysis and synthesis. The context and needs that give rise to process systems are examined along with the development of complex flowsheets. Techniques for the decomposition of large, complex systems to smaller problems are developed. While computer simulation skills are developed to facilitate the design, creation and evaluation of complex systems, costing and financial analyses are introduced to determine the feasibility of new developed processes.

CHEE3020 continues to build foundational process engineering skills, including mass and energy balance ﾠskills (developed during CHEE2001),ﾠ knowledge of ﾠprocess equipment and knowledge of process control. CHEE3020 ﾠintroduces more advanced analytical approaches, tools and methodologies and applies these skills for analysis, interpretation and optimisation of larger and/or more complex processes involving many unit operations and unit processes. Students learn to apply advanced analytical skills at multiple levels ranging from whole processes toﾠ the operation of individual units within these processes, including how changes in the operation of key equipment ﾠimpacts the broader process.ﾠ

A key aspect of CHEE3020 is the application of computer-aided process flowsheeting and simulationﾠ tools to facilitate process analysis, and using this software along with other core engineering skills to explore ﾠthe design and/or optimisation of processes with consideration of economic, social ﾠ& environmental impacts. In CHEE3020 students will learn techniques for the development of new process flowsheets that are both practical and realistic, including the identification and evaluation of process variations; as part of this work students will identify process selection criteria and refine decision making skills.ﾠ

Additional timetable information
Mentor meetings between student project teams and the teaching team are scheduled twice during semester and generally occur outside scheduled class time (usually in Week 4 and Week 10).
course_aims: The aims of this course are to:

learning_outcomes.description: Develop appropriate process engineering diagrams and perform mass and energy balances on complex flowsheets.
Use a systematic approach for analysing and developing chemical engineering processes.
Use a systematic approach for synthesising and developing chemical engineering processes.
Use Aspen Plus simulations for process development.
Carry out costing and economic analyses to determine the feasibility of a new process.
Work effectively in an engineering project team to develop new chemical engineering processes.
Communicate process information at a professional standard in both written and oral formats.

assessment_summary.due_date: Project report 6/09/2024 5:00 pm; Project presentation will be organised in Week8 Tutorial section.

assessment_details.title: Project 1 Report and Presentation
assessment_details.due_date: Project report 6/09/2024 5:00 pm Project presentation will be organised in Week8 Tutorial section.
assessment_details.other_conditions: Peer assessment factor. See the conditions definitions
assessment_details.task_description: This is a project to apply process analysis skills. The product to be examined is selected by the team with a project report delivered in week 7 (20% of total assessment) and a presentation delivered in week 8 (5% of total assessment). Peer assessment on team criteria will determine a Peer Assessment Factor (PAF) for individual team members. This task has been designed to be challenging, authentic and complex. Whilst students may use AI and/or MT technologies, successful completion of assessment in this course will require students to critically engage in specific contexts and tasks for which artificial intelligence will provide only limited support and guidance. A failure to reference generative AI or MT use may constitute student misconduct under the Student Code of Conduct. To pass this assessment, students will be required to demonstrate detailed comprehension of their written submission independent of AI and MT tools. Project hurdle is applied in this course. Read full task description Hide full task description
assessment_details.submission_guidelines:
Online submission of project reports via Blackboard.
Deferral or extension
You may be able to apply for an extension .
The maximum extension allowed is 28 days. Extensions are given in multiples of 24 hours.
Late submission
Assessments must be submitted on or before the due date. Late submissions of assessment items will only be accepted if approval for late submission has been obtained prior to the due date.
Penalties Apply for Late Submission
Refer PPL Assessment Procedure Section 3 Part C (48)

assessment_details.title: Aspen Plus Simulation
assessment_details.due_date: Week9 tutorial section will be used for this assessment.
assessment_details.task_description: In this assessment, students will be given a process engineering scenario and example process data. Students are required to work individually to draw block flow diagrams, build and run Aspen simulations and present simulation results. This assessment task evaluates students' abilities, skills and knowledge without the aid of generative Artificial Intelligence (AI) or Machine Translation (MT). Students are advised that the use of AI or MT technologies to develop responses is strictly prohibited and may constitute student misconduct under the Student Code of Conduct. Read full task description Hide full task description

assessment_details.title: Final Exam
assessment_details.task_description: Individual final exam tests understanding of all aspects of the course material from lectures, tutorials and project work. No written materials are permitted. No electronic aids are permitted (e.g. laptops, phones). This assessment task evaluates students' abilities, skills and knowledge without the aid of generative Artificial Intelligence (AI) or Machine Translation (MT). Students are advised that the use of AI or MT technologies to develop responses is strictly prohibited and may constitute student misconduct under the Student Code of Conduct. Exam hurdle is applied in this course. Read full task description Hide full task description

learning_resources.library_resources: Find the required and recommended resources for this course on the UQ Library website.

learning_activities.learning_period: Multiple weeks From Week 1 To Week 13 (22 Jul - 27 Oct)
learning_activities.topic: Lectures A series of Lectures covers the principles required for process analysis, synthesis, simulation, costing and financial evaluation. Lectures are recorded and will be available in the course blackboard page. A semester plan showing the weekly lecture topic is available on the course blackboard page. Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03, L04, L05

learning_activities.learning_period: Multiple weeks From Week 1 To Week 13 (22 Jul - 27 Oct)
learning_activities.topic: Tutorials The Tutorials aim to put lecture materials into practise and assist with development of practical skills for process analysis, synthesis, simulation, costing and financial evaluation. Students will be trained on using process simulation solftware during tutorial sections. Tutorial sessions are NOT RECORDED, however worked solutions are provided for tutorial problems. A semester plan showing the weekly tutorial topic is available on the course blackboard page. Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03, L04, L05, L06, L07

learning_activities.learning_period: Multiple weeks From Week 1 To Week 13 (22 Jul - 27 Oct)
learning_activities.topic: Project work Students work in teams on projects to apply the principles of process analysis, synthesis, simulation, costing and financial evaluation. Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03, L04, L05, L06, L07

policies_and_procedures: University policies and procedures apply to all aspects of student life. As a UQ student, you must comply with University-wide and program-specific requirements, including the: Student Code of Conduct Policy Student Integrity and Misconduct Policy and Procedure Assessment Procedure Examinations Procedure Reasonable Adjustments - Students Policy and Procedure Learn more about UQ policies on my.UQ and the Policy and Procedure Library . School guidelines Your school has additional guidelines you'll need to follow for this course: Safety Induction for Practicals Course guidelines Safety Induction for Practicals Anyone undertaking courses with a practical component must complete the UQ Undergraduate Student Laboratory Safety Induction and pass the associated assessment. Specific instructions, usage guidelines and rules for each of the undergraduate laboratories will be delivered as part of each course. In some cases, students may be required to attend a specific face-to-face laboratory induction/training session. Learning activities
